content.text: Debt Allocation in Sprint and PI Planning

Sprint and program increment (PI) planning are the moments when a team's capacity commitments are made concrete. Debt reduction that is not allocated in sprint planning does not happen — it competes with feature work, bug fixes, and urgent operational tasks, and it always loses unless capacity has been explicitly reserved for it. The allocation decision is not about how much time the team has "left over" after feature work is planned; it is a first-class capacity commitment that is made before feature work is scheduled, not after.

attrs.alt: Sprint capacity allocation: 60% feature work, 20% debt reduction, 15% bug fixes, 5% ops, with P1 debt pre-empting P3/P4 allocation

content.text: The 20% debt allocation is the baseline recommendation for a Claude integration team in active development. It is not universal: a team that has just launched a major integration may temporarily reduce the debt allocation to 10% to focus on immediate feature completions; a team addressing a P1 debt backlog may temporarily increase it to 30%. The baseline exists to set a floor that prevents debt from accumulating faster than it is addressed under normal conditions.
Sprint-Level Debt Selection

Selecting which debt items to schedule in a given sprint requires balancing priority, effort, and context. A P1 item always enters the sprint if it exists. For the remaining debt capacity, the selection considers whether the item can be completed within the sprint (debt items that require architectural changes spanning multiple sprints are not suited for the 20% capacity slot; they need a dedicated planning effort). The selection also considers sprint context — a sprint that involves major changes to the system prompt is a good time to also schedule prompt debt reduction items, because the engineer is already working in that area and the context switch cost is low.

The is_over_capacity check prevents a common sprint planning pattern where the debt allocation grows to fill whatever time is available after feature work is scheduled. If the P1 items already consume the full 20% allocation, adding P2 items means the sprint is over-committed on debt. The correct response is to either reduce P2 item scope, split a P2 item across two sprints, or explicitly accept that the P2 item will slip — but all three decisions should be made consciously in planning, not discovered mid-sprint.
PI Planning: Debt Roadmap Across the Quarter

Program increment planning sets capacity commitments across a quarter. At the PI level, debt planning involves three activities: committing to clear all P1 and P2 items within the PI, identifying any P3 items that have been open long enough to require commitment, and flagging any debt items that require a dedicated effort beyond the 20% allocation — typically items that require architectural changes too large for a single sprint slot.

The p1_items_cleared_by_end invariant enforces that P1 debt items do not carry over between PIs. A P1 item that carried over from the previous PI is a signal that the team consistently over-commits in PI planning or that P1 items are being misclassified — if an item truly warranted P1 assignment (high-impact, compounding), it cannot be allowed to remain unresolved for an entire quarter. A carryover should trigger a retrospective question: was the P1 classification correct? Was the effort estimate wrong? Was the capacity allocation too low? Was the item deprioritised in favour of feature work?
Negotiating Debt Allocation Under Feature Pressure

Sprint planning for Claude integration teams frequently involves tension between debt allocation and feature commitments, particularly when product stakeholders are tracking feature delivery velocity. The 20% debt allocation looks like 20% of feature delivery that is being "lost" to maintenance work; the correct framing is that the 20% allocation is preventing a compound interest payment — the debt that is not addressed today will cost more to address next quarter, when it has become more entangled with newer code and the context for the original decision has faded.
Stakeholder Concern
Evidence-Based Response
Negotiation Outcome
"We need the 20% for a critical feature"
Show the P1/P2 backlog and estimated cost if deferred for a sprint
Defer P3/P4 only; maintain P1/P2 allocation
"The debt doesn't seem to affect users"
Show compounding items and interest rate projection for next quarter
Agree on reduced allocation (15%) for this sprint with review next sprint
"Why can't engineers fix this as part of feature work?"
Explain that co-mingled debt work lacks visibility and ownership
Agree to co-mingle for P3/P4 only; P1/P2 remain discrete items
"We just launched; there shouldn't be debt yet"
Show items registered during pre-launch sprint and retrospective
Agree 10% for first post-launch sprint; return to 20% after T+30 review

The negotiation outcomes in the table preserve the P1/P2 allocation in all scenarios. The only flexibility is in the P3/P4 allocation and whether low-priority items are addressed as discrete sprint items or co-mingled with feature work. This is the correct place to flex: P3/P4 debt has low urgency and stable interest rate, so deferring it for one sprint does not meaningfully compound. P1/P2 debt has high urgency or compounding interest; deferring it for one sprint to accommodate a feature commitment is a decision to pay more later, which should be documented as an explicit trade-off rather than an implicit agreement.
Measuring Debt Allocation Effectiveness

Reserving 20% of sprint capacity for debt reduction is only valuable if the allocation is resulting in actual debt reduction. Measuring effectiveness closes the loop: the team can observe whether the register is shrinking, whether the debt items being completed are the right ones, and whether the quality improvements from debt reduction are visible in the product metrics. Without measurement, the allocation becomes a ritual — capacity is reserved, items are completed, but the register's health does not improve because the completed items were low-priority while high-priority items continue to accumulate.

The total_reduction metric compares register size before and after the sprint. If the register is not shrinking despite debt capacity being used, the team is completing debt items but new debt is being registered faster than it is resolved. This is a signal that debt prevention measures are insufficient — the team needs to address the source of accumulation, not just the backlog. The allocation_utilisation check catches two opposite problems: an under-utilised allocation (the team planned debt work but did not complete it, or the items were too large) and an over-run allocation (the items took significantly longer than estimated, which will cascade into feature delivery slippage).
Debt Allocation in SAFe and PI Planning Contexts

Teams operating within SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) or similar PI planning structures face an additional challenge: debt allocation must be planned not just at the sprint level but at the PI level, and it must be coordinated with dependent teams and program-level objectives. A Claude integration team that depends on a shared API gateway, a shared data pipeline, or a shared model management service may have debt items that require work from those teams as well. PI planning is the moment to surface these cross-team dependencies.

The uncommitted_blockers method surfaces the PI planning failure mode specific to shared-infrastructure debt: a team commits to resolving a P1 or P2 debt item that requires a platform team to make a change, but that platform team has not been included in the PI planning conversation and has not committed to the dependency. At the sprint level, the debt item cannot start because the dependency is not ready; the debt either slips or the team implements a workaround that creates additional debt. The can_start_in_sprint check prevents this by making the dependency explicit: a cross-team debt item cannot be scheduled in a sprint until the other team has committed to its side of the work.
Tracking Debt Allocation Over Multiple Sprints

A single sprint's debt allocation decisions are not sufficient to evaluate the quality of the process. The pattern across multiple sprints reveals whether the team is consistently delivering on its debt commitments, whether the allocation is the right size for the team's debt accumulation rate, and whether the debt composition is improving (more P3/P4 and fewer P1/P2 over time). Tracking across sprints also reveals seasonal patterns: debt allocation tends to be deprioritised in sprints leading up to major releases and then overloaded in the sprint immediately after, which creates allocation volatility that is harder to sustain than a consistent baseline.

The allocation_volatility metric catches the sprint-to-sprint inconsistency pattern. A team with high volatility — alternating between 5% debt work in feature-heavy sprints and 40% in catch-up sprints — is not getting the compound benefit of consistent maintenance. Consistent 20% allocation addresses debt gradually before it becomes acute. Volatile allocation allows debt to grow during low-allocation sprints and then requires high-effort catch-up sprints, which disrupt feature velocity. The standard deviation threshold of 0.25 is calibrated to flag volatility that is causing functional problems without flagging minor sprint-to-sprint variation that is within acceptable bounds.
Summary

Debt allocation in sprint and PI planning requires treating the 20% debt capacity as a first-class commitment made before feature work is scheduled. The SprintDebtPlan structure allocates P1 items unconditionally and fills remaining capacity with P2/P3 items selected by priority and sprint context. The PIDebtPlan commits to clearing all P1 items within the PI, scheduling P2 items, and identifying strategic items that need dedicated architectural work beyond the standard allocation. The p1_items_cleared_by_end invariant prevents P1 debt from carrying over between PIs. The negotiation framework preserves P1/P2 allocation in all scenarios and limits flexibility to the P3/P4 tier, where deferred work does not compound.
